title: AEE 2657: What Happened When You Were About to Leave?

sentences.en: This is an All Ears English Podcast, episode 2657.
What happened when you were about to leave?
Welcome to the All Ears English Podcast, downloaded more than 200 million times.
Are you feeling stuck with your English?
We'll show you how to become fearless and fluent by focusing on connection, not perfection.
With your American host, Michelle Kaplan, the New York Radio Girl, and Aubrey Carter, the IELTS Wiz, coming to you from Arizona and New York City, USA.
To get real-time transcripts right on your phone and create your personalized vocabulary list, try the All Ears English app for iOS and Android.
Start your seven-day free trial at allearsenglish.com forward slash app.
On today's episode, Aubrey and Michelle answer a listener question about we were about to leave and we went to leave.
What's the difference?
Find out today to make your stories much more engaging.
Have you ever met someone whose English wasn't perfect but they sounded incredibly fluent?
That's because fluency isn't about perfection, it's about flow.
It's about expressing ideas clearly, responding naturally, and staying engaged in the conversation, and generally feeling connected.
If you're ready to move beyond accuracy and into true fluency, start by finding your current level.
Take our free two-minute fluency quiz today.
Go to allearsenglish.com slash fluencyscore.
That's allearsenglish.com slash f-l-u-e-n-c-y-s-c-o-r-e.
Hello, Michelle.
How are you?
Hi, Aubrey.
I am doing well.
How are you?
Well, I accidentally turned my tattoo purple.
So you guys can see it's purple if you're looking on YouTube.
It was this beautiful brown henna, but it was fading.
But that's a great accident.
I mean, I love the hidden mess up of trees.
It's a happy accident.
I think it's fun.
I do too.
I do too.
But I do have a question for you.
Do you ever go to leave your house and then remember or realize that you forgot something like maybe car keys or wallet, purse, phone, whatever it may be?
Constantly. Yesterday, I went to leave and my car wouldn't start.
So I just realized I didn't have the keys in the car because now it's a push start.
So if my keys are in my purse, it'll just start.
But I was like, well, I just got to go back inside and get the keys.
That happens pretty much constantly.
What about you?
Yeah, of course.
Oh my gosh, especially with kids, like little kids, then someone is asking, oh, I needed a snack.
And you realize, oh, I didn't bring this on tan lotion.
I didn't bring this.
I didn't bring that.
I forgot.
In fact, the reason my henna was fading is because I kept forgetting to bring a long sleeve shirt.
And I was going to be outside playing volleyball or pickleball or something.
And if I could wear a long sleeve shirt, it would be fine because it's not supposed to be in direct sunlight.
But then when I went to leave, I didn't have the long sleeve.
And so it made my henna fade really fast.
Sad. Oh, but I got touched.
I thought they were black.
They were purple.
But you know what?
Happy accident.
Happy accident.
Yeah. So this is interesting.
We say this a lot.
I went to leave or I always go to leave.
And this happens.
We got a great question on YouTube about this.
And this question comes to us from Sophia, who we absolutely love.
Sends us amazing questions.
Thank you, Sophia.
Aubrey, would you read the question for us?
Absolutely. And this might have actually been emailed in.
I didn't realize it was Sophia.
Hello, Sophia.
Shout out and thank you for all of your amazing questions.
She said, hi, Lindsay, Michelle and Aubrey.
I was listening to your episode, Don't Let English Vocabulary Breakdown on You.
And I had a quick question about one part.
Aubrey said, we went to leave and it wouldn't start.
It's the same thing.
I went to leave and my car wouldn't start.
I'm a little confused about went to leave.
Does it basically mean we were about to leave?
Is there any difference between those two?
Thanks so much.
I love the show and learn a lot from you guys.
Best Sophia.
Always amazing questions.
I love this when you guys hear one thing in an episode.
You're like, why did they say it that way?
That makes for great follow-up questions, follow-up episodes.
Yeah, send us your question.
That's a great way to do it.
You just listen to us and you think, wait a second.
I thought it was said this way.
It's great.
Yes. And you can send those to support at allearsenglish.com or comment them in your rating, your review.
Also, YouTube, on our YouTube channel, Allears English, you can comment on any video, a question that we could cover on the podcast.
Yep. And as always, guys, hit the follow button wherever you're listening to Allears English so you never miss anything from us.
So to answer Sophia's question, we went to leave is very similar to we were about to leave.
They both describe the same time in a story when you're about to leave somewhere and they can often be used interchangeably.
Right, exactly.
So I mean, Aubrey, when could we use these phrases?
So telling a personal story, you're showing the sequence of events.
You're in the process of leaving when something unexpected happens.
You're putting the audience in your shoes and helping them experience it with you, but they are a little bit different.
So let's first share about we went to leave.
How is this different, Michele?
Yeah. So we went to leave focuses more on the attempt to leave.
Okay. So we were trying to leave, but it was stopped by something.
It also sounds slightly more casual or conversation and a little bit like conversational.
Totally. It got conversational.
Yes. And it sounds more like a story is unfolding.
So like it's a little more engaging.
It might pique someone's curiosity a little more.
This makes you a better storyteller, right?
You are pulling the listener in.
It's more engaging.
I do this a lot.
I'll switch to present tense and I'll use a phrase like this.
Both of those are strategies for making a story more interesting for making the listener feel like they were there with you.
Yeah. So for example, Aubrey, it sounds like this has happened to you.
We went to leave and the car wouldn't start.
Uh-oh.
That's not good.
Yes. And that might be because you forgot the keys.
It might be because the battery died, right?
But we went to leave and we were stopped.
We were prevented in our attempt to leave by something.
Yes. And that's never, never good.
So or I went to leave, then I realized I had no idea where I was going.
Yeah. I do that all the time.
I'll start driving and I forget to put the address in the GPS and then driving.
I can't do it while I'm driving.
So then at a stoplight, because in Arizona, it's illegal to have your phone in your hand at all while you're driving, even stopped at a stoplight.
So I'm like watching to make sure a cop doesn't pull up next to me while I'm trying to put the address in my GPS and my stop.
I'm safe.
The car's not moving, but I could still get a ticket.
So I have to be covert.
Or she went to leave, but the door was locked for some reason.
Uh-oh.
That sounds a little scary.
Well, that's happened to me when I've locked myself out of my car.

It's like you think the car's unlocked so you go to leave and then the car's locked and you're like, so luckily, hopefully that's happening to you when you're home and you just go find the keys and unlock the car.
But this says, I'm sure all of our listeners, this often, especially with older cars, that'll happen where you, you know, I went to leave and the door was locked and I couldn't get into my car.
Right. Right.
You have to call a locksmith to come and break into your car for you.
Yeah. I think I'm thinking of it in a scarier way.
Like she went to leave.
I'm thinking of you're already inside some place.
Oh, you're thinking you got abducted.
Yeah. Like I got you in a basement, Michelle.
That went dark really fast.
I don't know.
I don't know what.
No, I don't know.
Yeah. Like I'm thinking of like a scary movie.
It's a true crime.
A scary movie.
Oh, great.
Let's hope none of you are in that situation, but it could happen.
Be ready.
Make sure no one locks you in anywhere.
Yeah, exactly.
But if they do, you can say, you can use this English.
All right.
That's not funny.
I was about to leave and I was locked in.
Okay. So we were about to leave.
This is a little different.
We do use these, but it's slight.
It's a nuance, but so this focuses more on the timing.
Something happened just before leaving.
It does.
It's more neutral.
It's a very subtle difference.
This is slightly less vivid.
It's still good for storytelling, but it doesn't necessarily mean that you were prevented, that something happened that stopped you from leaving.
It's like we were about to leave and we realized something, something, right?
You weren't really prevented.
Just something happened as you're about to leave.
Right. So for example, we were about to leave when it started raining.
So I'm thinking I was with my kids once in the car.
We were trying to meet my husband at this event.
It was a terrible storm and I kept on checking the forecast on my phone and it would say rain continuing for 10 minutes.
And so I'd say, okay, let's just wait for 10 minutes.
And then you get to seven minutes and it would say for 18 minutes the rain is continuing and it just kind of like getting right.
And we never, we, and then, and then there was one time where I thought, okay, it's done.
And so we were about to leave when it started raining.
So then it just started again and we never made it.
Oh, that's so funny.
We're not going.
We're not going.
It's raining.
No. Yeah.
Or it sounds like we don't want to leave, but my boss called me over.
So maybe you're about to leave work, but then your boss is like, I just need to talk to you really quickly and two hours later.
No, probably it would be a quick conversation and then you'd be able to go.
Or they were about to leave when they realized they forgot their passports.
Okay, well it sounds before they left.
Exactly. Exactly.
You don't want to realize that at the gate, at the security when you go to show your passport to the security agents.
How far do you live from the airport, Aubrey?
30 minutes.
A 30 minute drive depending on traffic.
I'm about the same.
It could be over an hour depending on traffic.
Yeah. So you don't want to have to turn.
I mean, every minute counts.
That's why you miss a flight.
Yeah. You have to go back for my passport.
Double check the passwords before you leave the house.
Yes. Starting your own business is a dream for so many people.

One more time, get your free trial at S-H-O-P-I-F-Y dot com slash A-E-E.
All right.
These phrases don't only have to be about leaving to go somewhere.
They can be about any action that was about to happen or got interrupted.
So went to, I went to call you, but my phone died.
He went to take a sip of my coffee and it spilled all over my shirt or we went to unlock the door and the key snapped in half.
That has happened to me before.
It's terrible because yeah, you can't get the little piece of metal out of the key.
You have to replace the lock, the worst.
Oh, and that's expensive.
And then there's was or were about to.
So I was about to text you when you called.
That can happen a lot.
And it's like, oh, we were thinking of each other, right?
Or I was about to fall asleep when my phone started ringing non-stop.
That's the worst.
I wanted a nap.
Stop calling me.
Yes. Or she was about to cook dinner when the power went out.
Oh, that's no good.
No, right.
I guess we're going out to eat kids.
I actually would have that would be that sad.
I'm like, oh, darn the power's out.
Let's go get pizza.
Yeah, right.
And now you I know you can get your pizza for free.
Exactly. Right.
So we have if you guys haven't heard this, my daughter won free pizza for a year and we have been taking advantage.
Every week we go and pick up our pizza.
They know her by name.
They're so cute.
Her name's Georgia, but she goes by Gigi.
And when she goes in, everyone there is like, Gigi, what's up?
And if anyone's like a new, a new employee, they're like, this is Gigi's art on the wall.
They like show the art that won her free pizza for a year.
She's like a celebrity there.
It is so fun.
That is really fun.
So all right, let's do a role play.
So here we are friends and I called you to chat.
All right.
Hey, I was just about to call you.
That well looks like I beat you to it.
How's your morning going?
Stressful. I was about to leave the house and I couldn't find my keys anywhere.
Oh no, I hate when that happens.
What did you do?
Well, I went to call my mom for the replacement.
But as the phone was ringing, I found them in my jacket pocket.
Classic. That happens to me all the time.
Yeah, but it didn't end there.
When I finally went to leave, the car wouldn't start.
Seriously? That's terrible.
Yeah, I ended up calling AAA and getting a jump start.
And this is interesting.
The one thing I had here, well, I went to call my mom for the replacement.
What I would say, I would call if I had to go to the dealership and get a new one made, I would call that a replacement.
But if my mom has an extra set of keys, I would say, well, I went to call my mom for her set of keys or for a spare key is what I would say instead of a replacement.
Yeah. Yeah.
Yeah. Good point.
Good point.
Let's go through this role play.
We used a bunch of these here.
I first said, hey, I was just about to call you.
Does that ever happen to you, Michelle, when you call someone and they're like, I was just about to call you or I was just texting you.
I was just typing a text to you.
That happens to me a lot.
Yeah. I would say maybe mostly with my mom.
But there are definitely friends who that happens with, but then it would probably more so be a text message.
I was just about to text you.
Yeah, for sure, because we don't call that much anymore.
Same. Yeah.
And then I said, oh, no, no, no.
Then, oh, I asked you, how is your morning going?
And you said stressful.
I was about to leave the house and I couldn't find my keys anywhere.
Uh-oh.
I know.
What is the weirdest place you have found your keys?
Mine were in the fridge once.
I couldn't find them anywhere.
I think I had grabbed, I don't know, something out of the fridge and accidentally set them in there.
Which seems crazy.
I definitely found weird things in the fridge before.
I don't know if like a kid's toy, they like go to grab something.
Yeah, exactly.
I remember when my son was really little, he got this little car he was absolutely obsessed with because, you know, how it goes for kids.
So it's a fun car.
Yeah. So, and then I remember we couldn't find it.
We couldn't find it anywhere.
And then my mom was visiting.
And of course, my mom is the one who ends up helping.
It was just in an oven mitt.
In an oven mitt?
Like in the drawer where you put the oven mitt?
Yeah, yeah.
And then inside the actual mitt.
Yes. That is really funny.
But your mom found it when she went to cook or something.
Yeah, I guess so.
And then I made a rule.
I remember like I would say that I don't want my kids to have any toys that are smaller than the palm of my hand.
You know, just like only bigger toys that I can see.
Nothing that can fit in an oven mitt.
Yes, exactly.
Exactly. That's so funny.
Okay. So then you said, yeah, I went to call my mom and I'd say for the spare key.
I went to call my mom for her spare key.
I think you said that.
And then when I finally went to leave, the car wouldn't start.
So yeah, this is really interesting.
These are subtly different.
It's really about whether you were stopped in being able to leave or not, or if something was just happening as you were leaving.
Yeah, definitely.
So really interesting stuff there.
Guys, head on over to episode 2653.
